us-politics.headline: US Shoots Down Plane Over Iran as War Costs Mount
us-politics.body:
The US military rescued an airman after shooting down a plane over Iran, Trump confirmed Saturday, marking a significant escalation in the ongoing conflict. The incident adds to growing pressure on the administration as the war enters a new phase.

The fighting is already hitting American consumers hard. Jet fuel costs have skyrocketed since hostilities began, forcing airlines to cancel flights and raise fares. Travelers are facing a squeeze with little relief in sight.

The economic fallout from the Iran conflict is spreading fast. Airlines warn the disruption could last months if fuel markets stay volatile.

sources.title: Jet fuel costs skyrocket amid Iran war, exacerbating crisis for airlines, travelers
Trump says U.S. military has rescued airman shot down over Iran

us-politics.headline: ICE Enforcement Sparks Clashes in Congress and Courts
us-politics.body:
Black Democrats are pushing back against ICE agents wearing face masks during arrests, drawing explicit parallels to historical tactics used to intimidate Black communities. Republicans argue the masks protect agents from retaliation by criminal networks.

A separate dispute broke out after Democratic Senator Chris Van Hollen claimed an illegal immigrant caused a crash while fleeing ICE. DHS pushed back hard, saying the facts contradict his account. Both sides are using the incident to press their broader immigration arguments.

Meanwhile, a federal judge blocked a Trump administration move that would have required colleges to certify race played no role in admissions decisions. The ruling is a setback for the White House's push to enforce its post-affirmative-action agenda on campuses.

sources.title: In fight against ICE facemasks, Black Democrats point to history
Judge blocks Trump bid to require colleges to prove race not considered in admissions
DHS slams Democrat Sen Chris Van Hollen claim, says illegal alien caused crash while fleeing ICE

us-politics.headline: Harris Eyes 2028 as Louisiana Parade Attack Injures Several
us-politics.body:
Kamala Harris has been traveling and making public statements that Fox News and other observers read as early groundwork for a 2028 presidential run. She has not confirmed any plans, but her schedule and rhetoric are drawing scrutiny from both supporters and critics sizing up the Democratic field.

Separately, several people were injured after a vehicle drove into a crowd at a parade in Louisiana. Details on the driver's motive remain limited. The incident follows a string of vehicle-ramming attacks at public events across the country in recent years.

sources.title: Kamala Harris' travels and comments clearly point to 2028
Several injured after vehicle drives into crowd at Louisiana parade

au-politics.headline: Australia scrambles to secure fuel supply amid shortage fears
au-politics.body:
Japan and other Asian nations have assured Australia that normal fuel supplies will continue, as Prime Minister Albanese works to head off a domestic shortage. The assurances follow a period of acute supply anxiety that prompted NSW authorities to fine nearly 100 service stations $1,100 each for displaying misleading prices at the pump.

Western Australia is tightening oversight in parallel, ordering all regional stations to report prices through the FuelWatch scheme from May. Farmers are caught in the middle - one told ABC he is struggling to put food on the table as fuel costs bite hard. The government faces pressure to show the supply chain is stable before the situation worsens.

sources.title: 'Struggling massively': Farmer finds it hard to put food on the table
Nearly 100 NSW service stations fined $1,100 each over misleading petrol prices amid fuel shortage crackdown
Asian countries assure Australia ‘normal supply’ of fuel will continue as Albanese focuses on averting shortages
Japan assures Australia will get 'normal supply' of fuel
All WA regional stations must report fuel prices from May as FuelWatch scheme expands

au-politics.headline: Sisters of slain officer to sue Victoria Police for negligence
au-politics.body:
The sisters of Senior Constable Neal Thompson, killed by Dezi Freeman, plan to sue Victoria Police for negligence. Their lawyer says the family feels abandoned by the force, and that systemic failures left Thompson exposed to a known threat.

The case will test police duty-of-care obligations to their own officers. Victoria Police has not yet responded publicly to the legal action. The lawsuit adds to broader scrutiny of how the force manages risk to frontline members, and comes as a separate incident in Tasmania left a man in serious condition after being shot by police.

sources.title: Sisters of officer killed by Dezi Freeman plan to sue police for negligence, lawyer says
'Abandoned': Sisters of officer slain by Dezi Freeman to sue Victoria Police
Man in serious condition after being shot by Tasmania Police

au-politics.headline: Murder arrest at Melbourne airport; ATV crash kills Adelaide girl
au-politics.body:
A man was arrested at Melbourne Airport attempting to board an international flight after allegedly murdering a woman. Police intercepted him before departure. The case raises questions about alert systems at border checkpoints.

Separately, a seven-year-old girl died after an ATV rolled over in Adelaide's north-east. The death is one of two fatal crashes reported on the same day, underlining ongoing concerns about the safety of all-terrain vehicles, particularly around children. Neither incident has a direct political dimension, but both are likely to prompt calls for tighter regulation.

sources.title: 7yo girl dies after ATV rollover in Adelaide's north-east
Man arrested attempting to board international flight at Melbourne airport charged with murdering woman

au-politics.headline: US airman rescued from Iran as nuclear diplomacy stirs
au-politics.body:
A US Air Force airman was rescued from Iran in a special forces operation, the same day Spectator Australia reported that Tehran has offered Washington a diplomatic olive branch. The timing is striking, though it is unclear whether the two developments are connected or whether the rescue complicates any back-channel talks.

The incident lands at a sensitive moment for US-Iran relations. Any deal would carry significant implications for the Middle East and for Australian foreign policy, given Canberra's close alliance with Washington. Neither the Australian government nor opposition has commented publicly on the rescue.

sources.title: Iran has offered Trump an olive branch
US F-15 airman rescued in Iran after special forces operation

world.headline: US-Israel War on Iran Enters Day 37 With Widening Fallout
world.body:
Now 37 days in, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran is escalating on multiple fronts. Iran claims it has downed two American aircraft, while the US confirmed rescuing a second pilot whose F-15E was shot down over Iranian territory - a recovery Trump called 'miraculous'. A looming deadline over the Strait of Hormuz is raising fears of a broader regional chokepoint crisis.

Iran struck Kuwait's power and water infrastructure as attacks on Gulf states continued, while Yemen's Houthis claimed a cluster missile strike on Ben Gurion airport. Thousands rallied in Iraq against the war. Back in Washington, Trump is under pressure to find an exit that doesn't look like defeat. Polling shows the conflict is deeply unpopular with the American public.

sources.title: US pilot from downed F-15E plane rescued in Iran: What we know
What we know so far about rescue of US airman in Iran
Kuwait’s power, water plants damaged as Iran keeps attacking Gulf states
Thousands rally in Iraq against ‘senseless’ US-Israel war on Iran
Trump seeks to end the war without it becoming ‘an abject failure’
Iran war: What is happening on day 37 of US-Israeli attacks?
Yemen’s Houthis claim cluster missile attack on Israel’s Ben Gurion airport
‘Miraculous’: US rescues second pilot downed in Iran, Trump confirms
Trump’s unpopular war on Iran
Iran war live: Iran claims downing of 2 US planes; Hormuz ‘deadline’ looms

world.headline: Satellite Blackout and Hormuz Tensions Signal Information War
world.body:
US satellite company Planet Labs has announced a blackout on imagery related to the Iran war, cutting off a key source of independent visual intelligence at a critical moment in the conflict. The move raises serious questions about transparency and the ability of journalists and analysts to independently verify battlefield claims.

Meanwhile, Iran signalled selective access to the Strait of Hormuz, saying Iraqi ships may pass freely as overall transits tick upward. The carve-out suggests Tehran is using the waterway as a pressure lever rather than imposing a blanket blockade - for now.

sources.title: Iran says Iraqi ships can pass Strait of Hormuz as transits tick up
US satellite firm Planet Labs announces blackout on war on Iran images

world.headline: Ukraine Strikes Russian Oil Infrastructure in Long-Range Drone Raids
world.body:
Ukraine launched drone attacks on Russian oil infrastructure, hitting a port in Primorsk and an oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod. Fires and fuel leaks were reported at both sites. The strikes mark another push by Kyiv to degrade Russia's energy revenue and logistics capacity deep inside Russian territory.

The attacks come as the broader war grinds on with no ceasefire in sight, and as global attention remains heavily focused on the US-Iran conflict.

sources.title: Ukraine hits port in Russia’s Primorsk, oil refinery in Nizhny Novgorod

world.headline: Holy Week Under Occupation as Gaza Siege Tightens
world.body:
Palestinian Christians in the West Bank and Gaza are marking Holy Week under severe Israeli movement restrictions, with access to Jerusalem's Church of the Holy Sepulchre sharply curtailed. The subdued observances reflect the broader suffocation of Palestinian civilian life now well into its second year of intensified conflict.

New footage emerged showing an Israeli military dog attacking a Palestinian man inside a mosque, drawing fresh condemnation. Separately, Israel's ongoing dismantling of UNRWA operations continues to strip away basic humanitarian infrastructure, with critics arguing the move is a deliberate strategy to deepen the humanitarian collapse in Gaza.

sources.title: Footage shows Israeli military dog attacking Palestinian man inside mosque
Israel’s attack on UNRWA is central to its genocide of the Palestinians
Under Israeli restrictions, Palestinian Christians mark quiet Holy Week

ai.headline: AI Pricing, Fakes, and Authenticity Collide in One Week
ai.body: Anthropic announced that Claude Code subscribers will pay extra to use OpenClaw, adding another layer to the increasingly fragmented AI pricing landscape. Meanwhile, a folk musician found herself targeted by AI-generated fakes and a copyright troll simultaneously - a preview of the legal chaos ahead for independent creators. The pressure is building from both directions. Andrej Karpathy published a public "idea file" for an LLM wiki, while The Verge reported on a growing demand for proof that creative work is human-made. Authenticity is becoming a selling point. Analysts tracking OCR and document AI agents note that practitioners are openly posting about accuracy failures, suggesting the gap between vendor claims and real-world performance is widening.

sources.title: A folk musician became a target for AI fakes and a copyright troll
LLM Wiki – example of an "idea file"
Anthropic says Claude Code subscribers will need to pay extra for OpenClaw usage
Really, you made this without AI? Prove it
Unverified: What Practitioners Post About OCR, Agents, and Tables

ai.headline: Linux 7.0 Cuts PostgreSQL Performance in Half
ai.body: An AWS engineer flagged a serious regression in Linux 7.0 that cuts PostgreSQL performance by roughly 50 percent, and a fix may not come quickly. The issue points to deeper trade-offs in the kernel's memory and scheduling changes, which can quietly punish database workloads even as they benefit other use cases. This is a significant operational risk for teams running PostgreSQL on modern Linux kernels in cloud environments. The report landed on Phoronix and quickly drew attention from the infrastructure community.

sources.title: AWS engineer reports PostgreSQL perf halved by Linux 7.0, fix may not be easy

ai.headline: Right-to-Repair and Digital ID Face Industry Pushback
ai.body: Tech companies are lobbying to weaken Colorado's right-to-repair law, one of the strongest in the US, by pushing amendments that would limit which devices and components fall under its scope. The effort mirrors industry tactics used against similar legislation in other states. Separately, Germany's eIDAS digital identity implementation drew criticism after documentation revealed it requires users to have an Apple or Google account to function - effectively handing two US platform giants a gatekeeping role over European digital identity infrastructure. Both stories reflect the same tension: governments writing rules that companies then quietly hollow out.

sources.title: German implementation of eIDAS will require an Apple/Google account to function
Tech companies are trying to neuter Colorado’s landmark right-to-repair law

ai.headline: Nvidia eGPUs on Arm Macs, AV2 Decoding Lands on Laptops
ai.body: Apple approved a driver enabling Nvidia external GPUs to work with Arm-based Macs, opening a path for users who want more graphics headroom without switching platforms. It is a narrow but meaningful win for hardware flexibility on a platform Apple has historically kept tightly controlled. On the codec front, the Alliance for Open Media demonstrated real-time AV2 decoding on consumer laptops, a milestone for the next-generation open video format. AV2 promises significantly better compression than AV1, and getting it to run in real time on everyday hardware removes a key barrier to adoption.

sources.title: Demonstrating Real Time AV2 Decoding on Consumer Laptops
Apple approves driver that lets Nvidia eGPUs work with Arm Macs

btc.headline: Bitcoin Faces Quantum Threat and Bearish Sentiment Surge
btc.body:
Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion market cap is drawing serious attention from cryptographers racing to harden the network against quantum computing attacks. Several initiatives are now underway to develop quantum-resistant cryptographic standards before the threat becomes practical, a timeline some researchers put within a decade.

Meanwhile, bearish chatter about Bitcoin on social media has hit a five-week high, according to Santiment data. Price stagnation is fueling the pessimism. Historically, elevated bearish sentiment has sometimes preceded price recoveries, as retail capitulation clears the way for renewed buying.

sources.title: Bitcoin bearish social chatter reaches 5-week high: Santiment
Bitcoin's $1.3 trillion security race: Key initiatives aimed at quantum-proofing the world's largest blockchain

btc.headline: Crypto's Token Glut Is Destroying Value, Analysts Warn
btc.body:
The crypto industry is confronting a structural problem: token supply is growing far faster than genuine value creation. New launches flood the market constantly, diluting capital and attention across thousands of assets with thin utility and weaker fundamentals.

Analysts describe the dynamic as existential. Projects race to issue tokens to fund development, but the resulting oversupply depresses prices and erodes investor confidence across the board. Without a meaningful shift toward value-backed issuance, the cycle risks becoming self-defeating.

sources.title: Crypto faces ‘existential’ token problem as supply outpaces value creation

btc.headline: Drift Protocol Hack Was Months in the Making
btc.body:
Preliminary findings into the $280 million Drift Protocol exploit reveal the attack was not opportunistic. Investigators say the perpetrator spent months in deliberate preparation, mapping vulnerabilities and timing the strike carefully.

The scale and sophistication of the breach put it among the more serious DeFi exploits in recent memory. It also renews pressure on protocols to invest in proactive security audits rather than reactive patches, as attackers increasingly treat major platforms as long-term targets worth sustained effort.

sources.title: Drift Protocol $280M exploit took 'months of deliberate preparation'

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Mushroom Galette
food.description: A free-form savory tart with caramelized leeks and shiitake mushrooms lacquered in white miso butter on a rough puff pastry base.
food.tip: Chill your pastry dough for at least 30 minutes before rolling — cold fat creates the flaky layers that make a galette worth the effort.

exercise.name: The Sunday Loaded Carry Circuit
exercise.equipment: Two kettlebells or heavy shopping bags filled with books
exercise.description: Cycle through farmer carries, suitcase carries, and overhead holds across a fixed distance, resting only between variations — brutally simple full-body conditioning that builds grip, core, and posture simultaneously.

learn.topic: The Ship of Theseus and Personal Identity Over Time
learn.description: The ancient paradox asks whether an object that has had all its components replaced remains fundamentally the same object — and when applied to human cells, memories, and beliefs, it becomes a surprisingly destabilizing question about who you actually are.
learn.why: On a quiet Sunday it's worth sitting with the idea that the person who woke up this morning shares almost no atoms with the child who first learned to read — and asking what, if anything, makes you continuous with that child.
